home.title: HowManyPrompts: how many AI prompts is an everyday action worth?
home.description: See the real cost of an AI prompt (electricity, water, CO₂) by comparing it to everyday actions. Sourced numbers, no agenda.

convertir.title: Convert my AI prompts into everyday actions | HowManyPrompts
convertir.description: Tell us what you did with AI today (text, images, videos) and see the concrete equivalent in electricity, water and CO₂.

comparatif.description: Google search vs AI query, downloaded image vs AI-generated image: orders of magnitude, side by side, honestly.

intro.lead: Spend a whole year of AI prompts.
intro.sub: {label}: that's already {cost} AI prompts. One prompt is tiny, but it's their number that adds up: stack everyday actions and see how few it takes to burn through a whole year of AI.

result.title: Recap
result.headline: You spent {phrase}!
result.shareText: My everyday actions cost as much as {phrase} 👀 HowManyPrompts

facts.f1: A text prompt ≈ 0.3 Wh: about an LED bulb on for ~2 minutes.
facts.f2: The cooling water for one prompt ≈ 0.3 mL: 5 drops.
facts.f3: A prompt's CO₂ ≈ 0.2 g: an email with an attachment weighs ~250× more.
facts.f4: A 5-minute shower is « worth » about 250,000 prompts in water.
facts.f5: A Paris → New York flight is « worth » millions of prompts in CO₂.

comparatif.title: Comparison: the classic way versus AI
comparatif.subtitle: The usual action against its AI equivalent, side by side. Static, sourced data, an honest verdict.
comparatif.bonusText: 1 shower = 167,000 prompts, 1 h of Netflix = 300 prompts… Play with the orders of magnitude yourself in the game.

links.combien: How many prompts is an action worth?

ia-vs-google.title: AI search vs Google search
ia-vs-google.short: Does a text prompt use more than a Google search? A sourced, numbered answer.
ia-vs-google.verdict: Today, a text prompt uses about as much as a Google search (~0.3 Wh). The famous « 10× more » comes from an old IEA estimate (2.9 Wh), now outdated: recent models have cut their consumption by ~10. Honest caveat: reasoning or multimodal queries remain far heavier.

faq.q1: Does ChatGPT use more than a Google search?
faq.a1: For an average text prompt, no: both sit around 0.3 Wh. The gap shows up mainly with reasoning, image or video queries.
faq.q2: Where does the « 10× more » figure come from?
faq.a2: From an IEA estimate of ~2.9 Wh per query, now outdated: recent models have cut consumption by about 10 (Google reports 0.24 Wh).
faq.q3: Can one AI answer replace several searches?
faq.a3: Often yes: a single answer synthesizes what would take several searches and page visits. In practice, the balance can even tip in AI's favor.

image-ia-vs-photo.title: AI-generated image vs existing image
image-ia-vs-photo.short: How much does an AI-generated image cost versus reusing an existing photo?
image-ia-vs-photo.verdict: Generating an image costs 20 to 40× a text prompt (~6 to 12 Wh), far more than reusing an image already online. It stays modest against a physical action (≈ a few minutes of oven), but it's the first real « jump » in consumption once you leave text.

faq.q1: How much does an AI-generated image use?
faq.a1: On the order of 6 to 12 Wh, i.e. 20 to 40 times a text prompt.
faq.q2: Is it worse than downloading a photo?
faq.a2: Yes: reusing an existing image only costs a data transfer (~0.1 Wh). Generation, by contrast, spins up GPUs.
faq.q3: Why does an image cost more than text?
faq.a3: Producing an image takes far more compute (thousands of « denoising » steps) than a text answer.

video-ia-vs-streaming.title: AI-generated video vs video streaming
video-ia-vs-streaming.short: Is generating a video with AI worse than watching Netflix? The numbers.
video-ia-vs-streaming.verdict: Generating 5 seconds of AI video uses as much as ~12 hours of streaming (~940 Wh versus ~77 Wh/hour). It's by far the heaviest item in consumer AI, and its consumption isn't linear (doubling the length quadruples the energy). Use with awareness.

faq.q1: How much does an AI-generated video use?
faq.a1: About 940 Wh for a ~5-second clip, an order of magnitude well above text or images.
faq.q2: Is it worse than watching Netflix?
faq.a2: For the same length, yes, by far: 5 s of generation equals ~12 h of streaming. But we watch hours of video and rarely generate more than a few seconds.
faq.q3: Why is AI video so power-hungry?
faq.a3: It generates many mutually consistent frames; the cost grows faster than the length (doubling the length can quadruple the energy).

prompt-vs-email.title: Asking AI a question vs sending an email
prompt-vs-email.short: Does an email pollute more than an AI prompt? A CO₂ comparison.
prompt-vs-email.verdict: A text prompt emits less than a simple email: ~0.2 g of CO₂ versus 4 g (plain email) to 19 g (with an attachment). Worth keeping in mind: our most mundane digital actions often weigh more than a question to AI.

faq.q1: Does an email pollute more than an AI prompt?
faq.a1: In CO₂, yes: a plain email (~4 g) or one with an attachment (~19 g) exceeds a text prompt (~0.2 g).
faq.q2: How much does an email emit?
faq.a2: On the order of 4 g of CO₂ for a plain email, up to ~19 g with an attachment (Berners-Lee / ADEME estimates).

sources.title: Sources & data
sources.intro: Every number on this site comes from one of these sources. Cross-referenced 2025-2026.

methodologie.title: Methodology
methodologie.intro: How we turn an everyday action into a « number of prompts ». Everything is honest and bounded by ranges: orders of magnitude, never false precision.
methodologie.brief: We start from an action's measured, sourced consumption, divide it by one prompt's consumption, and get its equivalent in AI prompts. Nothing more.

flow.s2: ÷ one prompt's cost (0.3 mL of water)

methodologie.costTitle: How much does an AI prompt use?
methodologie.costLead: For an average text prompt: one inference of a large language model (LLM) of the GPT-4o class.
methodologie.costNote: We count only inference (usage), not training. Reasoning queries (~15-40 Wh) and image (~6-12 Wh) or video (~940 Wh/5 s) generation cost far more, see the glossary.
methodologie.formula: prompts = action's consumption ÷ one prompt's consumption
methodologie.formulaNote: That's the whole mechanic of the game: each added action is converted into its prompt equivalent, then compared to an AI « budget ».
methodologie.virtual: For food and goods we count the production footprint (« virtual » water and CO₂), not just the tap water at the meal. A burger « costs » mostly through farming (~2,500 L of virtual water), not the glass of water beside it: a toilet flush (direct water) and a steak (virtual water) don't measure the same thing.
methodologie.scalesLead: The budget we try to « spend » matches a real AI consumption:
methodologie.scalesNote: We don't assume everyone uses AI: these budgets rest on actually observed usage (ChatGPT ≈ 2.5 billion prompts a day), not on world population. In the game you aim at one of the annual budgets (You, 100 people, all generative AI); « ChatGPT in 1 day » is shown here as a reference point.
methodologie.rangeTitle: Why ranges instead of one exact figure?
methodologie.rangeLead: Sources vary (power mix, measurement method, scope). We keep a credible median and show the range. Two telling examples:
methodologie.rangeFlight: Flight: depending on whether radiative forcing is included (×~1.9 for DEFRA/ADEME; CO₂ only for ICAO) and one-way vs round-trip, the figure varies twofold.
methodologie.rangeBeef: Beef: from ~60 to ~99 kg CO₂e/kg depending on the farming type.
methodologie.rangeEnd: Showing the range is refusing false precision.
methodologie.train1: It matters, and it's far from negligible: training an LLM can use on the order of a gigawatt-hour of electricity, sometimes much more, the equivalent of hundreds of homes for a whole year.
methodologie.train2: It is, however, a one-off cost amortized over the hundreds of billions of inferences that follow; per prompt, it becomes small again. The site only quantifies inference (the everyday usage each of us controls), but training is very much part of AI's overall footprint: we say so clearly rather than hide it.
methodologie.q1: How much does an AI prompt use?
methodologie.a1: For an average text prompt: ~0.3 Wh of electricity, ~0.3 mL of direct cooling water, ~0.2 g of CO₂. We count only inference, not training.
methodologie.q2: How do you compute an action's « cost in prompts »?
methodologie.a2: We divide the action's consumption by a prompt's: prompts = action value ÷ prompt cost. A 50 L shower ≈ 50,000 mL ÷ 0.3 mL ≈ 167,000 prompts.
methodologie.q3: Why 12,000 prompts per person per year?
methodologie.a3: It's a heavy-use assumption (≈ 33 prompts/day). The larger scales don't multiply by world population: they rest on AI's real consumption (ChatGPT ≈ 2.5 billion prompts/day).
methodologie.q4: What are « virtual » water and CO₂?
methodologie.a4: For food and goods we count the production footprint (« virtual » water/CO₂), not just tap water.
methodologie.q5: Why ranges instead of one exact figure?
methodologie.a5: Because sources vary. We keep a credible median and show the spread: honest orders of magnitude, not false precision.
methodologie.a6: Costly but one-off, amortized over hundreds of billions of requests; per prompt, it becomes small. The site quantifies usage (inference), while acknowledging training is part of the overall footprint.
methodologie.outro: Every value is dated and linked to its source on the Sources page.

apropos.title: About
apropos.lead: An informative site to put AI's consumption back into perspective, with the numbers to back it up.
apropos.purpose1: HowManyPrompts is purely informative: it puts the real consumption of a generative-AI prompt back into perspective. We set it against everyday actions, and even a simple Google search, to give concrete orders of magnitude rather than gut feelings.
apropos.purpose2: The goal is neither to demonize AI nor to oversell it. Large language models (LLMs) are tools that save time, already woven into hundreds of millions of people's daily lives; like any technology, they use resources. Understanding that footprint, without doom or denial, is what lets you use them clear-headed.
apropos.data1: All the data comes from public, verified sources: public agencies (ADEME, US EPA / DOE / EIA, EEA), research institutes and scientific papers (Water Footprint Network, Our World in Data after Poore & Nemecek, arXiv), the International Energy Agency (IEA), and official figures from AI companies (Google, OpenAI, Epoch AI).
apropos.data2: When sources diverge, we keep a credible median and show the range: honest orders of magnitude, never false precision. Every value is dated and linked to its source.
apropos.perspective1: An average text prompt uses roughly 0.3 Wh, 0.3 mL of water and 0.2 g of CO₂, about the energy of a Google search, and a few drops of water. On its own, it's tiny.
apropos.perspective2: But the unit isn't the whole story: multiplied by billions of requests a day, the consumption becomes significant at a global scale. Both are true at once, and that's exactly what this site helps you picture.
apropos.debateIntro: On AI's impact, two camps coexist, and each has solid arguments. We lay them side by side.
apropos.green1: The unit impact is small: ~0.3 Wh, comparable to a Google search (Google, OpenAI, Epoch AI).
apropos.green2: Efficiency is improving: newer models use less per request (Google: 0.24 Wh).
apropos.green3: Concrete benefits: productivity, medical-diagnosis support, climate research, accessibility.
apropos.green4: Compared with streaming, meat, cars or flights, AI stays modest per person.
apropos.orange1: The scale effect is real: ~2.5 billion prompts/day; data centers already account for ~1 to 1.5% of global electricity and are growing fast (IEA).
apropos.orange2: Reasoning models, and especially video, cost far more (up to ×1,000).
apropos.orange3: Water and local water stress around data centers (UC Riverside, Water Footprint Network).
apropos.orange4: Jobs, bias, privacy, concentration of power: up to ~40% of worked hours could be affected (WEF, ILO, Stanford HAI).
apropos.jobsTitle: AI and jobs: a transformation, not a novelty
apropos.jobs1: As with every major revolution, industry then the web, some jobs change or disappear while others emerge. Generative AI follows the same path. Studies (WEF « Future of Jobs », OECD, PwC) point mostly to a transformation of tasks rather than outright replacement: the tool augments the expert more than it replaces them, wherever judgment, responsibility and human context stay decisive.
apropos.position1: Neither starry-eyed technophiles nor technophobes. We believe that staying aware of how AI evolves, how it's used and what it costs is the best way to benefit from it, without naivety or fear. This site gives the numbers; everyone draws their own conclusions.
apropos.transparency1: Building this site (AI-assisted research and design) cost a modest order of magnitude, the equivalent of a few hours of TV. We show it too, for consistency: we don't ask of others a transparency we don't apply to ourselves.

footprint.label: This site's footprint
footprint.method: This site used AI for in-depth research and a bit of development. All in, building it adds up to about 250 Wh, 0.25 L of water and 170 g of CO₂.
footprint.perVisit: And each visit? A few tenths of a Wh on lightweight static hosting: negligible, even with lots of traffic.

glossary.metaTitle: Glossary: Wh, virtual water, radiative forcing… | HowManyPrompts
glossary.metaDescription: The key terms to understand the cost of an AI prompt and everyday actions.
glossary.title: Glossary
glossary.intro: The key terms to understand the numbers on this site, and AI in general.
glossary.outro: A term missing? Every value cited is sourced on the Sources page.

links.combien: How many prompts is an action worth?

generativeAI.def: A family of AI that produces content (text, image, audio, video) from a prompt. Large language models (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini) are its most common form.

llm.def: A neural network trained on massive text corpora to predict the next token. It powers chatbots; its footprint mostly comes from inference, on every prompt.

prompt.def: A request sent to an AI model. Site reference: an average text prompt ≈ 0.3 Wh, 0.3 mL of water and 0.2 g of CO₂.

token.def: The smallest unit of text a model handles (≈ ¾ of a word in English, often less in French). Models read, generate and « bill » by the token: the more tokens, the more inference consumes.

inference.def: The stage of using an already-trained model: each prompt triggers one inference. That's what we quantify here, not training.

training.def: The model's initial learning phase, very energy-intensive, but a one-off, then amortized over billions of requests. The site quantifies inference, not training.

hallucination.def: When a model generates false but plausible information. It's the main reason human verification remains essential.

contextWindow.def: How much text a model can take into account at once (a conversation's « memory »). The fuller it is, the costlier the inference.

parameters.def: The internal « settings » learned during training, counted in billions. As a rule, the more parameters, the more capable a model is… and the more power-hungry.

multimodal.def: A model able to handle several data types (text, image, audio, video) within a single request.

agent.def: An AI that chains several steps autonomously to complete a task (so, several inferences). Useful, but it multiplies consumption compared with a single prompt.

reasoning.def: A model that « thinks » (generates intermediate steps) before answering. Far costlier than a text prompt: ~15 to 40 Wh per request, i.e. 50 to 100× a standard prompt.

imageVideo.def: Producing media costs far more than text: an image ≈ 6–12 Wh (20–40× a text prompt), a video ≈ 1,000× (~940 Wh for a ~5 s clip).

wh.def: A unit of energy: running a 1 W device for 1 h. An LED bulb uses ~10 Wh per hour.

kwh.def: 1,000 Wh, the unit on your electricity bill. An electric oven uses ~2 kWh in an hour.

datacenter.def: The building that houses the servers where AI runs. It uses electricity to compute and water to cool down.

gpu.def: The graphics processor: the component that actually runs AI models. Powerful and power-hungry, it dominates consumption at inference.

pue.def: The ratio of a data center's total energy to the energy actually useful to the servers. ~1.1 (very efficient) to 1.5+: the rest goes mostly to cooling.

twh.def: 1 billion kWh. It's the scale of national or global consumption, where AI « weighs » at a global level.

virtualWater.def: The water used to produce a good (farming, livestock, manufacturing), not just tap water. A burger thus « contains » thousands of liters of virtual water.

directWater.def: The water evaporated on site to cool a data center's servers. It's the « official » figure reported by Google (~0.26 mL/prompt) or OpenAI (~0.32 mL).

wue.def: Liters of water used per kWh consumed by a data center. Used to estimate a prompt's « direct » water.

waterStress.def: When water demand exceeds the locally available resource. A key issue around some data centers built in dry regions.

co2e.def: All greenhouse gases expressed as their CO₂ impact, so they can be added up.

radiative.def: The extra warming caused by flights at altitude (contrails, other gases). DEFRA and ADEME include it (×~1.9); ICAO counts CO₂ only, hence flight figures that vary twofold.

gridIntensity.def: The grams of CO₂ emitted per kWh of electricity. Highly variable by country (low with nuclear/renewables, high with coal); ~480 g/kWh as a global average.

lca.def: A method that counts « cradle-to-grave » impact: manufacturing, use and end of life. It explains why an object's footprint exceeds its use-phase consumption alone.

scopes.def: The emission perimeters: direct (1), from purchased electricity (2), and everything else in the value chain (3).

orderMagnitude.def: A number's approximate « size » (×10, ×100, ×1,000). On uncertain data, it's more honest and more useful than false precision.

medianRange.def: When sources diverge, we keep a credible central value (median) and show the spread (range), never a single, falsely precise figure.
